_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt:
Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' — the resource economy that defines the Cariboo and rural BC.

CALIBRATION — use this ladder. Trade-press journalism about forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture should score well even when it's equipment-, market-, or operations-focused rather than policy-focused — a story about a piece of farm machinery or a mining operation update is a real fit, not a 0:
- 80-100: BC/Cariboo-specific forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture stories (mill closures, beetle kill, BCTS, Cariboo ranchers and water rights, local mine operations, Cariboo Cattlemen events).
- 55-79: Canadian resource-economy trade journalism without a BC tie — Western Producer or Northern Miner-style coverage of farm equipment and machinery, harvest technology, livestock markets, mining operations and exploration, AgTech, forestry practices, sawmills, or labour/commodity stories in the resource sector.
- 30-54: Broader agriculture, forestry, ranching, or mining news (e.g. international resource-industry stories, general farm economics, climate impacts on working lands) with clear working-lands relevance but no Canadian/BC tie.
- 15-29: Tangential resource-sector mentions (e.g. a general business, policy, or science story that touches briefly on forestry/agriculture/mining) or local Cariboo civic news unrelated to working lands.
- 0-14: No connection to forestry, ranching, mining, or agriculture.

Strong fits include: BC and Canadian forestry journalism (mill closures, lumber markets, beetle kill, harvest operations, silviculture, BCTS, old-growth policy), ranching and livestock stories (water rights, grazing management, cattle markets, drought impact on ranches), mining and resource extraction (safety, new operations, Indigenous consultation, supply chains, exploration updates), agriculture in rural Canada (crop insurance, grain prices, farm economics, food sovereignty, farm equipment and machinery), and the technology helping these industries adapt — precision forestry, AgTech, drone surveys, IoT in agriculture, mine automation, sawmill modernization, climate adaptation on working lands. Also strong fits: labor and workforce stories in resource industries, environmental stewardship within working landscapes (not pure conservation, but how ranchers/loggers manage for sustainability), and science with direct implications for forestry, agriculture, or extractive industries. Penalise heavily (score 0–14): international geopolitical and war news with no resource-economy connection; consumer tech and gadget reviews; pure wildlife entertainment without agricultural or working-lands relevance; crime and judicial news unrelated to resource industries; financial market and stock ticker stories. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.content_html:

Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, including warming, pollution, biodiversity loss, and ocean acidification.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Monument to the Hippies in Ibiza, Spain
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

This sculpture by Ció Abellí shows a father, with long hair and a typical hippie appearance. He walks with his little daughter who looks the same way. At their feet is a world map with the places in the world where the hippie communities were concentrated: San Francisco, Amsterdam, Kathmandu, Goa, and Ibiza. The monument pays tribute to the hippie movement that has played an important role in the development of Ibiza.

The bronze sculpture group is the three-dimensional materialization of an image taken in 1973 by photographer Toni Riera. You might think that it reflects a scene that took place in Ibiza, but it actually happened in a park in Amsterdam.

Riera was only 18 years old, and one weekend in Vondelpark, he saw two strangers: a father and his daughter. He snapped a photo that would become his best-known work, though he didn't know it at the time. During that time, Toni Riera first traveled to Ibiza and started working at Pachá washing glasses, and Ricardo Urgell, the owner of this famous nightclub, hired him as a photographer. On the walls of Pachá, he portrayed his version of some of the most important characters of the 20th century.

In the 90s, Urgell wanted to relaunch the place and give it an appearance more typical of the island's beginnings, as the hippie community that it once was in the 70s, and asked Riera for a photo that represented that image. The photographer searched through his negatives and found that old photo from 20 years ago taken in Amsterdam. Needless to say, that image of Pachá would end up becoming an icon of hippie Ibiza and at the same time meant a relaunch of the famous nightclub. In this way, a grateful Urgell commissioned the sculptor Ció Abellí to create a bronze statue that represented the photograph, and after that he donated this sculpture to the city of Ibiza, where, since its inauguration in 2016, we can see it immortalized in the port of the Ibizan capital.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Umtanum Creek Footbridge in Ellensburg, Washington
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Yakima (pronounced "YAK-uh-maw") Canyon, where the Yakima River traverses a water gap through Manastash Ridge, is an obvious thoroughfare and was used by Native Americans for thousands of years, and then by Euro-American trappers and settlers into the 19th century. It was also an obvious route for a railroad, and the Northern Pacific Railway was built through it in the early 1880s, along the west bank of the river. (The railroad is still in active use and is now part of the BNSF system.)

Railroading was an extremely labor-intensive business well into the 20th century, and camps were built along the right-of-way to house the trackwalkers, signal men, section gangs, and other workers. In 1885 one such camp was built here at Umtanum Creek, where the eponymous creek joins the Yakima. The camp even boasted a school and a post office by the early 20th century.

When in 1925 the automobile highway was built through the canyon on the east shore of the river, cable suspension bridges were built across the river from the railroad camps to provide highway access. Evidently the original bridges were wide enough to be passable to Ford Model As. In the 1960s, the Washington Department of Fish and Wildlife and the Bureau of Land Management took over the day-to-day management of the bridge, but only in 1987 was the bridge deck narrowed to make it accessible to pedestrians only.

With the continuing rise of automotive travel, it proved no longer necessary to station crews on the railroad right-of-way, and by the 1970s the last of the camps had been removed.

The cable suspension bridges were also removed, with the sole exception of the Umtanum Creek bridge. It was still used by recreational users to access the west side of the river, but because of age-related deterioration concerns were being raised about its safety. Meanwhile, efforts were underway to have the bridge declared a national historic site. Finally, Umtanum Creek Bridge was restored in early 2021, with attention paid to preserving the structure's historic values.

items.title: [ScienceDaily] Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.

items.title: [Kottke.org] Watch 1969’s Apollo 11 Moon Landing “Live!”
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

57 years ago today, on July 20, 1969, Neil Armstrong & Buzz Aldrin landed on the Moon and went for a little walk. For the 18th year in a row, you can watch the original CBS News coverage of Walter Cronkite reporting on the Moon landing and the first Moon walk on a small B&W television, synced to the present-day time. Just open this page in your browser today, July 20th, and the coverage will start playing at the proper time. Here’s the schedule (all times EDT):

4:10:30 pm: Moon landing broadcast starts
4:17:40 pm: Lunar module lands on the Moon

4:20:15 pm - 10:51:26 pm: Break in coverage

10:51:27 pm: Moon walk broadcast starts
10:56:15 pm: First step on Moon
11:51:30 pm: Nixon speaks to the Eagle crew
12:00:30 am: Broadcast end (on July 21)

Set an alarm on your phone or calendar! Also, this works best on an actual computer but I think it functions ok on phones and tablets if necessary.

Back in 2018, I wrote a bit about what to look out for when you’re watching the landing:

The radio voices you hear are mostly Mission Control in Houston (specifically Apollo astronaut Charlie Duke, who acted as the spacecraft communicator for this mission) and Buzz Aldrin, whose job during the landing was to keep an eye on the LM’s altitude and speed — you can hear him calling it out, “3 1/2 down, 220 feet, 13 forward.” Armstrong doesn’t say a whole lot…he’s busy flying and furiously searching for a suitable landing site. But it’s Armstrong that says after they land, “Houston, Tranquility Base here. The Eagle has landed.”. Note the change in call sign from “Eagle” to “Tranquility Base”. :)

Two things to listen for on the broadcast: the 1201/1202 program alarms I mentioned above and two quick callouts by Charlie Duke about the remaining fuel towards the end: “60 seconds” and “30 seconds”. Armstrong is taking all this information in through his earpiece — the 1202s, the altitude and speed from Aldrin, and the remaining fuel — and using it to figure out where to land.

items.title: [Atlas Obscura] Cerro de los Ángeles. Remains of the Old Sacred Heart in Madrid, Spain
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Situated in the metropolitan area south of Madrid, the Cerro de los Ángeles (Hill of the Angels) has been considered for a long time, the geographical center of The Iberian Peninsula, and  a strategic point from ancient times.

Centuries later, it became an important pilgrimage site to its sanctuary, which was constructed because an image of the Virgin Mary was discovered here.  For all these reasons, in this place, on May 30, 1919 was solemnly inaugurated by King Alfonso the 13th the monument of the Sacred Heart of Jesus, work by Aniceto Marinas . During the years prior the Spanish Civil War, it was a very crowded place by conservative and bourgeois society. Meanwhile it was stirred up a deeply anticlerical movement, including violent acts against nuns and priest and burning down of churches by anarchist groups. At the beginning of the war, the entire area fell within the Republican side. From that point on, the die was cast, so Communist and anarchist militiamen, came here and killed the five young men in charge of its custody. Then, they renamed the place as “The Red Hill”. Then they also decided to destroy the monument of the Sacred Heart, symbol of what they considered fascists and reactionary forces. However, prior to it, On July 28, 1936, a strange performance of shoot the image of Jesus was staged. The well-aimed shots struck around the heart, but none made much of a dent.

The  image of the "shooting" of the Sacred Heart had been considered a clumsy manipulation by rebels in this parallel propaganda war. However the curious photograph is totally real.  It is actually a part of a seven seconds sequence filmed by a cinematographic team that accompanied the militiamen with the premeditated aim of immortalizing the pantomime  in order to show their contempt for the Catholic Church, which the militiamen considered "an accomplice of the repressive factional forces". That sequence was broadcasted on the British news program British Paramount on August 18, 1936 and an image of that "firing squad" was reproduced in the London Daily Mail with the caption "The Spanish Reds, war on religion". The next target was to complete the demolish of  the monument, firstly manually, trying to break down the 900 tons of the column and the statue of Christ and later with a steel cable tied to a tractor, but the monument resisted, even breaking the wire. The surrounding people arrived in masse with mallets, hammers, and chisels; but even leaving it damaged, it was not until using three dynamite charges that the sculptural group could finally be demolished. When he finally jumped into the air, someone yelled, "The bearded man is down!" The Popular Front press celebrated it on its pages with the headline "The disappearance of a nuisance". When the Civil War finally ended, the Franco Government returned it to its original name and ordered the construction of a replica in the same place where the old Sacred Heart was placed. The works of the new sanctuary began in 1944 and finished in 1965, according a project by the architects Pedro Muguruza (author of the Valley of the Fallen) and Luis Quijada. The author of the image of the Sacred Heart of Jesus, of 11.5 meters (37.7 feet ) 2.5 meters (8.2 feet) taller, was Aniceto Marinas who had already created the previous one and the figures on the pedestal were the work of Fernando Cruz Solís. The remains of the old Sacred Heart were moved to a nearby space, right opposite, where the deteriorated base of the old monument is still standing and is shown to the public.

items.title: [Kottke.org] Goofy Oversized Wicker Costumes
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Artist Lewis Prosser describes himself on Instagram as an “absurdist basket-maker”. His costumes for a project called Making Merrie reflect that self-description.

Making Merrie explores the material culture of folk theatre. Inspired by mummers’ plays and masked traditions along the Wales/England border, Making Merrie combines craft, performance, and language to reflect on cultural heritage and exchange.

Mummers’ plays are traditional folk performances with roots over 500 years old, often tied to Christmas and New Year. Full of humour and spontaneous revelry, these plays were staged in streets, homes, or pubs by amateur troupes, telling simple stories of combat, death, and miraculous revival. Unlike the religious Mystery Plays, mummers’ plays are secular, carnivalesque, and performed for community fun.

The project features large-scale wicker costumes, handcrafted using regional willow basketry techniques, highlighting basketry as an essential human skill we’re at risk of forgetting—a skill that, if lost, means losing part of what it is to be human.

(via colossal)

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] As Sudan’s war crosses borders and millions are made refugees, could Ethiopia’s plan solve ‘humanity’s migration issue’?
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Amid fears of fresh bloodshed, the country is hoping its offer of education and employment can provide a model for other nations to follow

It didn’t matter that he was so frail he could barely walk. Rahmatalla Abdeen knew if they found him he would be shot. For two days, the 65-year-old had clung to the back of a moped as it navigated an area teeming with militia.

The Rapid Support Forces (RSF) had seized this corner of Sudan after crossing the border from Ethiopia. Abdeen was lucky: on 17 May, he became the last known refugee to cross into Ethiopia from Sudan.

items.title: [The Guardian Global Development] Prickly pears, avocados and lemons: how rainwater harvesting is feeding Mexico City’s gardens and homes
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

Thousands of low-income households are benefiting from a simple system that captures and filters rainfall from the city’s roofs

Walking up a steep hill to a small informal settlement of about 45 houses in Cristo Rey, above San Gregorio Atlapulco in Mexico City, Ana Aparicio Bahena arrives at her simple home. As she walks through her lush, verdant garden, she points out plants such as the prickly pear, cactus leaves with tiny, hair-like spines, and picks blackberries hanging from a bush, handing them to her two daughters.

“I can grow vegetables,” says Aparicio Bahena proudly, holding up a small onion. “I have avocados, lemons, potatoes … I never used to be able to grow my own food. It’s wonderful.”

items.title: [Kagi Small Web] EU against Meta
items.content_html:
🌾 ✏️

* EU regulators have accused Meta, the company behind Facebook and Instagram, of failing to tackle the risks of its "addictive design" on the physical and mental health of users.*

This is a very important problem, and one of the reasons to refuse to use those systems. But if the EU is serious about protecting users from them, it should take firm action -- take a big bite out of the problem, not a little nibble. Use of a recommendation engine should not be enabled by default.

I think that a platform should not be allowed to use or suggest any particular recommedation engine.
